# Data Portal Product Review (Pre-read)

# Data Portal Product Review (Pre-read)

Authors: [David Lin](mailto:david.lin@doordash.com) (Product) | [Jacopo Himberg](mailto:jacopo@wolt.com) (Eng)  
Type: Product Brief \- Leadership  
Read time: \~11 min

| Early  Product Vision Doc : [Data Portal & Authoring Product](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1OdcaRljfTT3NJsgUvwiBGXw9iH9uaPr6Qvvab1TEPWY/edit?tab=t.9xvktebxxp05#heading=h.4yj1ntpmvifx) |
| :---- |

![][image1]

*Data Portal [prototype](https://www.figma.com/make/MztA50vJemJX2Me5M57lFW/Data-Portal-2.0---Top-AI-bar?node-id=0-1&p=f&t=zjFn8kQpIdvuUxua-0&fullscreen=1)*

## **Context**

There are about 1,500 employees across S\&O, data scientists, and data engineers who are customers of our data platform. These customers use data on a daily basis to help make decisions, ship features, and grow the business. Today, these customers use dozens of tools to discover and author data which gives them a fragmented experience. Furthermore, we know that data discovery and authoring are high friction tasks and that improving the tooling will unlock material efficiency gains. 

Data Portal vision is to improve the discovery and authoring of data through these key themes

* A consistent UX layer for all data discovery and authoring tools  
* Leveraging AI across all workflows  
* Enable discovery and authoring tools through other agents such as Cursor

## **Discussion questions**

#### **1.) Should Data portal focus on data discovery before authoring?**

We believe Data Portal should focus on discovery first because all data customer personas are searching for data assets as part of their workflows. Discovery features such as keyword search are also the most feasible to build so we can ship faster and test our hypothesis earlier. 

We aim to prioritize building data authoring UX on the Pedregal stack which will be available later in 2026, but are open to discussing this sequencing with the team.

![][image2]

#### **2.) How will Data Portal make data asset discovery better?**

For example, how can Data Portal help S\&O and Data scientists find the right dashboard or data table among a large pool of certified and non-certified data?

What data should we allow to be searchable?

|  | All data available | Certified data \+ deemed relevant \[recommended\] | Data from new Pedregal ecosystem |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Team perspective | Would have too much irrelevant data that has little meta data for us to rank relevancy on, leading to poor value to users | Ideal approach as long as we can build up a catalog of relevant data through programmatic / manual effort \- i.e. last month we created [golden dashboards](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1Mb0odLjG5fycwOK_NhkO4tley7Xunox5TmQbelIQGHY/edit?tab=t.0#heading=h.kczrskbv96je) across company that Ax/DE teams in each domain helped define | Data too limited in near term for us to create a valuable product experience |

#### **3.) Which other customers could we add to our list of design partners?**

Today, these 3 customers have been our design partners. As design partners, we will dig into their use case, prototype solutions, and iterate. We’re happy to hear suggestions of other customers to include. 

| Use case | Opportunity | Customer |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Finding the most accurate data asset | Data asset search \- ask questions, explore data assets with trust signals | The Canada S\&O CX experience team [Mahira Arif](mailto:mahira.arif@doordash.com) |
| Getting ad hoc answers | AI search  | Consumer Ax [Alyssa Wisdom](mailto:alyssa.wisdom@doordash.com) |
| Visualizing and leveraging widgets | Metrics widgets and metrics viewing | Consumer Ax [Hao Lin](mailto:hao.lin@doordash.com) |

#### **4.) Will every data tool be part of the Data Portal?** 

Data portal will provide both entry points vs. native functionality for other data tools. We want Data Portal to be the unified entry point to access all data tools, but some of those tools will be natively built in Data Portal whereas others will just be linked 

* For example, we don’t see Data portal as a place to do native deep dive analysis tool but instead it should link to BI tools  
* For example, we don’t believe Data portal should build native support for pre-pederegal authoring flows such as ETL express, but will still follow the principle of unification and provide a guide on how to use legacy tools and a links

#### **5.) Is the vision of data portal to become an in-house BI tool?**

* Data portal and BI tools will complement one another, not replace. The main JTBD of data portal are discover data assets, stay on top of metrics you care about, and authoring new data. BI tools will still be the place for other JTBD such as deep dives, building sophisticated company dashboards, and more.  
* For example, Data Portal widgets will focus on quick visualizations to help team members track trends, and do shallow cuts while BI tools will still serve as a deep analysis tool.  
* Widgets in data portal will be powered by the metrics layer, providing direct access to the highest quality data in a very controlled way  
* We expect this feature, coupled with the AskAI one, to help satisfy basic data needs as well as follow up questions of a large amount of users who won’t need to access the BI tool necessarily anymore

#### **6.) What will happen to tools such as datahub and AskAINetwork?** 

* The data portal will act as a UX layer to unify data discovery tools such as AskAINetwork so users don’t have to use one tool for AI search and another for catalog search.  
* See more on data hub [here](#bookmark=kix.jvo1yg9ebu64)

![][image3]

* Datahub usage has started to decline, especially since the introduction of AskDataExplorer, suggesting there is less PMF with datahub. We believe the hybrid search of Data Portal will more effectively address pain points of users.  
* DataHub will continue to be for the time being a database of all the data, lineage and processing steps across DoorDash’s data landscape, however its usage as entrypoint to understand the data environment in the day to day of most of the teams is expected to drop substantially. We expect certain types of users such as Legal to still rely on DataHub for accessing, inspecting and extracting  the full data lineage.

**Problems and opportunities**

### **Discovery**

**Problem \#1**: **Finding the right data asset** \- It’s hard to discover the right data (tables, dashboards, metrics) because there are too many tools to choose from and tools don’t help users narrow down thousands of results to find which is the right asset.  
**Customer**: The Canada S\&O CX experience team [Mahira Arif](mailto:mahira.arif@doordash.com)  
**Use case**: Team has questions such as “what is SMB share of homepage impressions in Calgary?” and will often need to manually deep dive into dashboards and tables to do deeper analysis. Finding which dashboard is the right one to use is difficult with many search results and few trust signals in existing tools

*Fragmentation of discovery tools*  
![][image4]  ![][image5]![][image6] ![][image7]   ![][image8]  ![][image9]         
AskDataAI agents      BI tools	             DB Querying          Slack, Glean     Datahub         Custom workbook 

*Tools give too many results and don’t help narrow down the right asset*  
  ![][image10]

**Problem \#2**: **Getting a quick answer for ad-hoc queries** 

* **Customer**: Core Consumer Ax team [Alyssa Wisdom](mailto:alyssa.wisdom@doordash.com)  
* **Use case**: The team will sometimes get asked ad hoc questions during a live meeting that are difficult to answer live because they are often tangential to the agenda. This leads to followup deep dives and new meetings which can be costly. See [more](https://docs.google.com/document/d/15oJLFUcOpar5jW-ZxOSPsHcylPjtZ-Pg_IxC-yIrfcs/edit?tab=t.0)

### **Authoring**

**Problem \#1:** Metrics can’t be used for dashboards without writing more SQL

* **Customer:** Consumer Ax team [Hao Lin](mailto:hao.lin@doordash.com)   
* **Use case \#1**: We roughly estimate that there are potentially 200 Ax team members spending 4000 hours a quarter re-writing queries for already computed metrics (UMP) to create a dashboard.  
* **Use case \#2:**  Also, Ax team members also have to re-write queries to join multiple metrics (UMP) they want to use for ML or other analysis

 Dashboard makers still need to write hundreds of lines of SQL to display already defined metrics  
![][image11]

**Problem \#2: We lack an authoring workflow and** **instead have** **a collection of authoring tools**. Customers will create their own authoring work flow from tool to tool instead of following a prescribed workflow where we can streamline tasks such as prototyping, getting sign-off from stakeholders, and more

* **Customer**: Most Ax / DE customers experience this  
* **Use case**: Creating a new metric, launching a new feature, building data for ML

   	

## **Opportunities**

### **AI search (AskAI) for ad hoc questions OR keyword search for asset discovery**

* Get quick AI answers to questions such as “why did merchant cancellation rates increase last week?”. If needed, drill down deeper in deep dive AI mode where experience turns into an agent conversation,  
* Manually deep dive into dashboards or tables by easily finding what dashboards, tables, and other data assets are the most accurate. Find information that helps determine accuracy such as author, usage, lineage, but also find social sentiment about an asset such as slack threads and wikis 

![][image12]

### **Allow simple visualization and re-use of metrics**

* Make metrics a drag and drop component that can be visualized or re-used very easily  
  * Metrics widget \- Customers can select any UMP metric, pick dimensions to filter, and visualize it in Data Portal   
  * Metrics viewer \- Customers who prefer to take the metrics to do ML or deeper analysis, can just output a table or query instead of a widget

*Metrics widget*  
![][image13]

*Metrics viewer*  
![][image14]

### **AI assisted data authoring workflows**

Users can declare what they want an AI will provide them a customized workflow to streamline creation of data

![][image15]

### **Personalized base on role type**

An executive should see a different data portal experience than a data engineer, so we’ll personalize the experience based on the employee's role. 

* Example, data engineers who are in the builder persona, will have easy access to authoring tools whereas S\&O and executives will have more prominent discovery features. 

![][image16]

## **Success criteria and impact**

### **Success criteria**

We want PMF with our customers. We will measure and goal against engagement and retention to drive towards that. Meanwhile, we also want to see that the quality of data analysis is improving in the system, so we track that more directly with % of queries using certified data.

| Criteria | Metric | Goal | Rationale |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Engagement | **WAU,  DAU** | WAU \> 50% of TAM DAU / MAU growing | Defined as taking an action in application and solving a task |
| Retention | **14, 84 day retention** | \> 30% long term retention | Suggests PMF |
| Quality data usage | **% of queries that leverage certified data  Today’s baseline:  45%**  | Increasing % of certified data used | Indicates accuracy and improved ecosystem |

## **Impact**

Still a WIP, but see [appendix](#bookmark=kix.p9lbo3bk0c6) for current estimations

## **Milestones**

Our first milestone is to launch the hybrid search because we see this as the simplest feature to build that addresses the most common pain point across all data users whether its executives, S\&O, data scientists, and data engineers. 

Next milestones will focus on known pain points for certain personas. Our last milestone will be to make Data Portal functionality available through MCP so other agentic workflows can easily access our authoring tools, search tools, etc. 

![][image17]

## **Appendix**

**Agenda**

* 1-2 mins: Dave  
  * introduction   
  * vision of Data Portal, main problems we want to solve  
* 2-5 mins: Jacopo   
  * How data portal supports the data platform engineering vision  
  * Etc  
* 5-7 mins: Jacopo / Dave Demo the prototype main flows  
* 35 mins: Discussion topics

### **Impact** 

We want to measure impact by understanding how many hours our users could save using our tools. Efficiency gains will translate into more velocity which ultimately ladders up to driving the business through better decisions and features shipped. Please note that the estimates are rough and are not final, but serve as a directional guide

Hybrid search (keyword only)

* Today: We estimate the 1500 data customers (Ax, S\&O, DE) will keyword search for \~1 data asset per week, taking 10 minutes  
* Improvement: hybrid search conservatively reduces that time by 50%  
* **Potential impact:** 1500x 5 minutes \= **125 hr of effort / week reduced** OR \~3 FTE

Metrics Widgets

* Today: We estimate about 200 Ax customers write up to 1 dashboard every 2 months or 0.125 dashboards / week. Each dashboard taking 20 hours to write.   
* Improvement: Widgets should be able to reduce 90% of that effort, saving 18 hours per dashboard created  
* **Potential impact:** 200 x 0.125 x 18  \= **450 hours of effort / week reduced** OR \~11 FTE

Metrics viewer

* Today: We estimate 1 hour of writing queries for analysis per Ax user / week  
* Improvement: We conservatively estimate a 50% improvement, or 30 mins of effort saved per week assuming 50% of the queries are built on existing metrics  
* **Potential impact:**  200 x 12 mins / week **\~80 hours of effort / week reduced OR** \~2 FTE

Other areas to be sized in future

* It is estimated that [30-50%](https://docs.google.com/document/d/15oJLFUcOpar5jW-ZxOSPsHcylPjtZ-Pg_IxC-yIrfcs/edit?tab=t.0) of the Ax workforce is spent on ad hoc’s today and we have not yet included the impact of our AI search on that  
* We have also not calculated the impact of building AI guided authoring workflows (ingest, ETL, etc)

**How can we improve upon the datahub experience?**

Customers say datahub doesn’t help them find the right dataset because it returns too many search results and customers can’t figure out which result is accurate. We also see customers moving towards AI to search for data assets while usage of datahub has declined over the same period (see chart below).

Our approach is a hybrid search that combines both the AskAI capabilities and an improved keyword search capability that focuses on fewer results and surfacing trust signals such as slack mentions, freshness score, etc.   
![][image18]

#### **Other customer feedback**

More generalized feedback from other customers (thanks for the partnership) 

* Some Ax customers have heavy AI workflows (ie, Cursor) and would prefer to use data portal functionality agentically ([Matt Heitz](mailto:matt.heitz@doordash.com) Biz Ops Ax)  
* Some S\&O customers run the same daily queries to monitor trends, which helped support the need for customized widgets  ([Neil Ratonel](mailto:neil.ratonel@doordash.com) S\&O)  
* Data engineers building dashboards would love to see golden dashboards most relevant to their role and org ([Nisar Ahmed Shaik](mailto:nisar.shaik@doordash.com)Data eng)

#### **Personas and JTBD**

![][image19]

### **Comprehensive JTBD and took evolution**

![][image20]

# PRD

# Product Requirements Document: Data Portal

![][image21]

# 1\. Introduction

There are about 1,500 employees across S\&O, data scientists, and data engineers who are customers of our data platform. These customers use data on a daily basis to help make decisions, ship features, and grow the business. Today, these customers use dozens of tools to discover and author data which gives them a fragmented experience. 

This PRD will outline the Data Portal product requirements and also provide context on the problem we’re solving for users, impact, and success criteria.

# 2\. Product vision

Data Portal vision is a single tool that all users of data at Doordash will use daily to make better and quicker decisions so that we can create a better product for our customers and our business

# 3\. Target users and JTBD

We aim to serve 4 personas of users at Doordash that include:

1. Leadership (executives, PMs, marketers)  
2. S\&O (business operators, analysts),   
3. Ax, (data scientists),   
4. DE (data engineers and product engineers).  

As shown in the diagram,  Leaders and S\&O personas tend to focus on consumption/analysis jobs that include finding data, answering ad hoc questions and more. Meanwhile data engineers and product engineers focus on creating data or dashboards. Ax personas tend to do both.

![][image22]

# 4\. Pain points

### **Discovery**

**Problem \#1**: **Finding the right data asset** \- It’s hard to discover the right data (tables, dashboards, metrics) because there are too many tools to choose from and tools don’t help users narrow down thousands of results to find which is the right asset.  
**Customer**: The Canada S\&O CX experience team [Mahira Arif](mailto:mahira.arif@doordash.com)  
**Use case**: Team has questions such as “what is SMB share of homepage impressions in Calgary?” and will often need to manually deep dive into dashboards and tables to do deeper analysis. Finding which dashboard is the right one to use is difficult with many search results and few trust signals in existing tools

*Fragmentation of discovery tools*  
![][image23]  ![][image24]![][image25] ![][image26]   ![][image27]  ![][image28]         
AskDataAI agents      BI tools	             DB Querying          Slack, Glean     Datahub         Custom workbook 

*Tools give too many results and don’t help narrow down the right asset*  
  ![][image29]

**Problem \#2**: **Getting a quick answer for ad-hoc queries**   
**Customer**: Core Consumer Ax team [Alyssa Wisdom](mailto:alyssa.wisdom@doordash.com)  
**Use case**: The team will sometimes get asked ad hoc questions during a live meeting that are difficult to answer live because they are often tangential to the agenda. This leads to follow-up deep dives and new meetings which can be costly. See [more](https://docs.google.com/document/d/15oJLFUcOpar5jW-ZxOSPsHcylPjtZ-Pg_IxC-yIrfcs/edit?tab=t.0)

### **Authoring**

**Problem \#1:** Metrics can’t be used for dashboards without writing more SQL  
**Customer:** Consumer Ax team [Hao Lin](mailto:hao.lin@doordash.com)   
**Use case \#1**: We roughly estimate that there are potentially 200 Ax team members spending 4000 hours a quarter re-writing queries for already computed metrics (UMP) to create a dashboard.  
**Use case \#2:**  Also, Ax team members also have to re-write queries to join multiple metrics (UMP) they want to use for ML or other analysis

 Dashboard makers still need to write hundreds of lines of SQL to display already defined metrics  
![][image30]

**Problem \#2: We lack an authoring workflow and** **instead have** **a collection of authoring tools**. Customers will create their own authoring work flow from tool to tool instead of following a prescribed workflow where we can streamline tasks such as prototyping, getting sign-off from stakeholders, and more  
**Customer**: Most Ax / DE customers experience this  
**Use case**: Creating a new metric, launching a new feature, building data for ML

   	

# 4\. Solution themes

## **4.1. Discovery focused on accuracy**

Best in class discovery that solves the pain points of data discovery as well as ad hoc analysis of data

## **4.2. Personalized by Persona**

To be the unified tool across all personas, we need to show different experiences for each persona. A leader wants to monitor trends and expects a data portal to be more proactive. Whereas a data engineer is more focused on authoring and monitoring data.

## **4.3. Authoring with AI**

To solve the problem of fragmented workflow and tools, we can use AI to understand the user’s authoring intent and map out a customized workflow for them

## **4.4. Notifications to stay ahead of issues**

Notifications are essential to make sure users are using accurate data instead of finding out too late 

## **4.5. MCP for power AI users**

Enabling data portal functionality through MCP is critical when targeting users who spend most of their time in AI development experiences, ie. Ax on the business operations side are using cursor almost daily to help write SQL and don’t want another separate tool

# 5\. Milestones

![][image31]

# 6\. MVP user stories

For MVP, we should focus on two customer types: (1) Consumer Ax, (2). Canada S\&O team.

# 

| User story | Detail | UI Sample | Priority |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| I’d like to see the golden dashboards for my organization when I open data portal, so I have easy access to something I use frequently | **Canonical or golden dashboards** shown for each customer by their organization (within Workday) See golden dashboards [here](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1Mb0odLjG5fycwOK_NhkO4tley7Xunox5TmQbelIQGHY/edit?tab=t.0#heading=h.kczrskbv96je)  **Display style and CTA:** Display these visually as a thumbnail for the user, but when tapped can open dashboard in BI tool of their choice: Tableau, Mode, Sigma or user can copy link | ![][image32] | P0 |
| I want to be able to find data without getting returned thousands of results I can’t sort through and also be able to access social cues about each result that can help me feel confident about the data without pinging the owner Customers: Hao Lin from Consumer Ax Ethan A from Canada S\&O See full customer discoveries [here](#bookmark=kix.gttn3i2i31l5) | **Keyword search bar with auto-complete** User should have auto-complete options shown when typing into search bar See [more]() on semantic vs. keyword  |  | P0 |
|  | **Results filtering** User needs to be able to filter for the data type they are interested in: Raw data Derived data (fact/dimension tables) Metrics Attributes (UPSS/X360) Dashboards Pre-pedregal vs Pedregal, see [more]()  **Sort results by following criteria,** lower to higher \# criteria  \= higher weighting Pedregal asset vs non-Pedregal Gold/Silver/Bronze Certification (Project Hubble) Has an author or co-authors documented Has overall documentation links Has lineage available L7 usage statistics (\# of queries) **Search history** See recently searched example in DataHub cloud for inspiration  ![][image33]  |  | P0 |
|  | **Data asset search result tap requirements**  Behavior: When a search result is tapped, there is a modal called “data asset page” that contains **Data asset modal display information should contain** Tab 1: Overview Pedregal vs non-Pedregal Gold/silver/bronze Authors/co-authors Documentation Tab 2: Social Social cues (see next requirement) Tab 3: Lineage Lineage Tab 4: Other Any other stats such as those show in datahub can be put into this other as they are not shown to be as valuable for customers  ![][image34]  |  | P0 |
|  | **Social cues tab in Data Asset page**  \# of Slack matches for this particular keyword result in specific slack channels such as ax-org-internal in L84 (Last 84 days) Snippet of Slack conversation where keyword appears Include name of message author  Also snippet is appable and deep-linked to thread \# of mentions of this keyword result in internal documentation (wikis, google docs) L84  Snippet of wiki/doc text where keyword appears  Include name of doc author Also snippet is tappable and deep-linked to document  ![][image35]  |  | P0 |
| As a consumer Ax or S\&O in Canada, I want to see my team’s most important metrics when I open data portal so that I can track trends and act accordingly | Every weekly business review (WBR) document has every team’s top metrics which we can display in data portal based on the team of the user logged in |  | P1 |

# 

# 

# 7\. Metrics and KPIs

# 8\. Customer journey examples

## **Keyword search**

Full notes of both discovery [here](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1CB91ely_C9pdbFLEROtPAznlTGXeOGKVZyp-7DH7S9s/edit?tab=t.0)

| Customer journey \#1: Find out what items are the most popular so the team can promote them for the 2025 World Series (Canadian team played a US team) |
| :---- |
| **Customer:** [Ethan Appadurai](mailto:ethan.appadurai@doordash.com) |
| **Team:** Canada S\&O team |
| **Overall team objective:** Help Canadian merchants grow their business |
| **Journey in detail:**  Part 1: Ethan maps out a strategy for the data Ethan defines “popular item” as something that is searched for and something that is purchased Ethan will find table A which is most searched items in Cx Ethan will find Table B which is most purchased items by Cx Ethan will then join Table A and B to get most searched and/or most purchased Part 2: Find Table A \- most searched items In this case, Ethan already has this table documented in his google doc which shows the table name, and meta data about table, ie. author, description of what each field means Part 2: Find new trusted table B that has sold items  Ethan starts to search for table B by using AskDataAI , entering search terms that are relevant to sold items in orders When Ethan gets table search results, he first looks at each table’s certification gold/silver/bronze (ie. Project Hubble) Ethan tends to keep searching over and over until the results contain some gold/silver/bronze results Ethan picks a table that has certification or if no table is returned certified, Ethan will ask DRIs for a table Part 2: Confirm that table B is correct Ethan will now go through a series of validations on table B in addition to having already validated the table as gold/silver/bronze First, try to understand the **definition** of table B.  He looks for documentation docs from the table author that define the table. For example, what is the definition of purchased? Is it purchased, delivered, and customer satisfied, or just checkout?  In another example, Ethan said that Table B could be an impression table for placements (ie. carousel or banner) in Cx app, but impression definition is loose. Is an impression de-duped already? Or does the same Cx viewing multiple times count everytime? Second, Ethan cares about **lineage and freshness**. What are the data sources, ie. ETLs powering it   When was it last updated? Then Ethan cares about **authorship** (1). Who is the author (2). Who are the co-creators? Regardless if Ethan understands Table B’s definition, lineage, freshness, and that it’s certified, Ethan will still cross-check his work with the author or co-creator of table B Part 3: Perform join and share information Ethan can now join Table A and Table B to get the most searched and / or most purchased items Ethan will then share to his manager for feedback Part 4: Add knowledge back into his own google doc Ethan maintains a google doc of tables that he uses and understands Each table will contain his notes on definition, ie. definition of purchase or impression, definition on lineage and freshness, and of course authorship. |

| Customer journey \#2 Figure out how many promotions are merchant funded or doordash funded |
| :---- |
| **Customer:** [Hao Lin](mailto:hao.lin@doordash.com) |
| **Team:** Consumer Ax |
| **Overall team objective:** Grow consumer engagement |
| **Journey in detail**  Part 1: Understand output format Example, looking to output a table with consumer\_id, store\_id, number of promotion orders funded by Mx, number of promotion orders funded by DD, etc Part 2: Search for data sources Next Hao searches for the data sources that may contain the above format Search option \#1: Fastest scenario Ask colleagues and other DRIs what **table** or **metric** to use, then Hao would simply find the previous query and copy it over as one CTE and be done.  Search option \#2: Use AI to discover tables Use Cursor with the AskAnalytics or AskData MCP to search for tables or metrics (below shows example of prompt to AI) Note, even if the AI returns a query, Hao is looking for the source tables in the query because he does not trust the AI’s returned query `i would like to have analaysis starting with the following queries: select a submarket, say Boston, collect all user impression data in the past x days (say 7 days, but make it configurable) with consumer_id, store_id, session_id, if the store is running a promotion campaign, the propopsed percentage off of the promotion, if the consumer end up placing an order from the store in the same session`  Search option \#3: Search on UMP for related metrics then reverse engineer the tables they use For example, I can always start a search like this if I want to know some promotion-related metrics. If I feel a particular metric could be useful, I would click into it, and then a couple more clicks to get the exact definition of that metric (query)  ![][image36]  ![][image37] ![][image38]  Part 3: Validate for options 2 and 3 only. Option 1 does not need this part Make decisions on whether to use the definition. This step could also involve **discussions with colleagues** to understand the context and possible caveats of the data, especially when I am not familiar with the data source.  Part 4: Write the final query At this time, I should have the definition for each of the metrics in output. The remaining task is to “join” them properly.  For example, if metric A comes from table A, and metric B comes from table B, we will need some common identifiers to relate them in the same table. depending on the business, it could be the consumer\_id, date, store\_id, etc, or a combination of some of them.  Note that such join keys may not exist in all tables. For example, if metric A and metric B should be joined by consumer\_id, which exists in table A, but table B only has device\_id, we will need to find a third table that could give us a mapping from device\_id to consumer\_id. This could make the work even more complicated.  |

# Data platform assets

# Data platform asset classification for Data Portal Discovery

Document objective: to determine what data assets Data Portal will be visible to and how that visibility changes with Pedregal

## **Data portal discovery objective**

Data portal discovery aim is to help data customers find data assets that help them answer questions about the business, visualize data in dashboards, and also run experiments and marketing campaigns. 

Today, Data discovery experience is weaker because we have too many different tools for discovery that can confuse the user and within each tool we don’t optimize for accuracy and instead focus on exhaustive lists of search results that users have to manually verify to be accurate

## **Data assets needed for a comprehensive discovery experience**

**The data assets we want to bring into the scope of Data Portal discovery** and how they change in Pedregal are below

| Pre-pedregal | Post-pedregal | Where this is located |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Raw data | Same | Datalake: Snowflake, Databricks Data warehouse: Snowflake \+Queryrunner (Pedregal) |
| Fact/dimension table | Derived datasets | Airflow |
| Metrics | Same | UMP |
| Attributes | Same | X360, UPSS |
| Dashboards | Same | Sigma, Mode, Tableau \+Thoughtspot (Pedregal) |

See the full data component to vendor / storage mapping here: [Pedregal Data Guide - November 2025](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1fwc_xODwq30qW9oas5Y-ggwZsLWTNWlG6FzTPURIsgs/edit?tab=t.zcozggm61vo4#bookmark=id.w38peiwglkob)

## **How do we treat pre vs post-pedregal data assets during discovery?**

When building Data Portal discovery, instead of looking at what we have available today, we can build an experience that solves for the underlying data assets of the future.

Our goal is to drive the engagement of data portal discovery which will be measured in WAU and DAU. In order to do so, we need to help users search on classic data sets **and** pedregal data sets when we launch Data Portal discovery which will be in H1. However, once the migration to Pedregal is completed and we are ready to deprecate most of the classic stack, we should be able to seamlessly shift our discovery to Pedregal data assets only

|  | Pre-pedregal (aka classic) | Pedregal transition phase | Pure pedregal |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Timing | Today | 2026 H1 | Late 2026 or even 2027 |
| Data assets in existence **available** | Classic data sets | Classic \+ Pedregal data sets | Pedregal datasets |
| Data portal phasing |  | Launch |  |

## **UX Treatment**

When users do a keyword search in Data Portal, we can show them all assets by default, but allow them to filter for Pedregal assets as shown below.

![][image39]

# Keyword vs semantic vs AI answers

# Keyword vs semantic search vs AI answers

**Context**: In milestone 0 we want to build the best in class data asset search capability, but we need to understand what customers will type into the search bar and what they are expecting

**Problem we’re solving: We want a unified search bar that automatically determines which of 3 search intents the user has based on what they type**

| Search type | Search intent | Example search query |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Keyword search (for data assets)  | Find data assets | “Global order volume fact table” |
| Semantic search (for data assets)  | Find data assets | “Table that contains promo carousels and CTR rates”  |
| AI answers (for full AI answers)  | Get answers or analysis  | “What is the merchant cancellation rate in Canada L7?” “Give me the average CTR for promo carousels in Canada L30” |

## **Customer feedback on keyword vs semantic search**

Keyword searches assume users know the name of the data asset they are looking for whereas semantic searches assume the user wants the search tool to infer what the actual table name is from the semantic search input. For example, if a user doesn’t know the name of the data asset and types “order fact table”,  a semantic interpretation may return results such as “purchases”, “transactions” which are semantically similar to orders

Below table is based on interviews done with two users,  1x data scientist and 1x S\&O analyst. 

| Search input | Search expectation | Probability Ax  | Probability S\&O | Insights | Example search queries |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Keyword   | Match keyword | 50% | 50% | Customers both say they usually know the metric or asset they need | Intent: find table or metrics that represent deliveries that have problems  Examples: *“defect”, “cancellations”*  Intent; find most searched item during a baseball game *Example: “gold/silver table for most searched items”*  |
|  | Match semantically | 5% | 15% | Customers don’t usually expect results that are different from what they searched But in more rare cases, will search  |  |
| Semantic phrase | Match semantically | 45% | 35% | Customers find this valuable when in **exploratory mode**, ie. “I have a hypothesis I want to prove, and am open to all ideas” | Intent: get defective deliveries data *"i want to be able to query orders that are identified as defect"*  Intent: unsure what keyword represents defective deliveries Intent: understand what customers are interested in at a particular time *“Give me tables/metrics that show me what restaurant orders and searches customers are making at x gametime”* |

## **Proposal for keyword vs semantic search**

Within our keyword search experience, we can handle two flavors

1. Traditional keyword search   
   1. User inputs one or more **keywords and potential modifiers** (ie. “*gold/silver table for most searched items”)*  
   2. Prioritizes keyword matches   
   3. Shows semantic matches as lower priority and visually separate from keyword matches and helps customer understand why these results are shown to build confidence  
2. Exploratory search experience  
   1. User inputs **semantic phrase**  
   2. We show a mix of keyword and semantic matches that are sorted by our existing algorithm mentioned in the PRD

## **~~Proposal for AI answers \[deprecated\]~~** 

**~~Context:~~**

~~AI answers should be given to users when their intent is an answer to a question or AI to do analysis for them.~~  

~~For example: “What is the merchant cancellation rate in Canada L7 days?” would be clear intent for an answer to a question~~

~~Meanwhile, “GIve me the average CTR of buy one get one off promos for merchant XYZ” would be a clear intent for analysis~~ 

**~~Proposal:~~**

~~When users are asking questions or asking AI agents for analysis give users a section that contains an AI answer similar to how google gives a gemini overview before giving them a list of relevant keyword results too.~~

Example: User asks a question  
![][image40]

Example: User is searching for a data asset

![][image41]

# Ax pain points stack rank

# Ax pain points stack rank

Authors: [David Lin](mailto:david.lin@doordash.com) (Product) 

## **Ax team DRIs**

| Ax org (director or head of level) | DRIs |
| :---- | :---- |
| CX ([Linsha Chen](mailto:linsha.chen@doordash.com)) | [Gloria Yang](mailto:gloria.yang@doordash.com)[Ruben Kogel](mailto:ruben.kogel@doordash.com) |
| Dx ([Jeff Law](mailto:jeff.law@doordash.com)) | [Ethan Zhu](mailto:ethan.zhu@doordash.com) |
| Mx ([Yuanyuan Cui](mailto:yuanyuan.cui@doordash.com)) | [Vamsee Gottipati](mailto:vamsee.gottipati@doordash.com), [Yikai Chen](mailto:yikai.chen@doordash.com), [Angeline Guo](mailto:qi.guo2@doordash.com)[Balaji Chandrasekhar](mailto:balaji.chandrasekhar@doordash.com)[Mike Linshi](mailto:mike.linshi@doordash.com)  |
| Biz Ops ([Matt Heitz](mailto:matt.heitz@doordash.com)) |  |
| [James Williams](mailto:james.williams@doordash.com) |  |
| [Lokesh Bisht](mailto:lokesh.bisht@doordash.com) |  |
| [Beth Schaffer](mailto:beth.schaffer@doordash.com) |  |
| [Meg Davy](mailto:meg.davy@doordash.com) | [Di Peng](mailto:di.peng@doordash.com)[Johnathan Postiglione](mailto:jpostiglione@doordash.com) [Jackson Chan](mailto:jackson.chan@doordash.com)[Nathan Shih](mailto:nathan.shih@doordash.com)[Sherry Xia](mailto:sherry.xia@doordash.com) |

## **Pain points stack ranked**

| Pain point | Description | Ranking |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **MX pain points** \- [full descriptions](#bookmark=kix.1odob1pzflyg)  |  |  |
| Ad-hoc queries are easily broken | Changing source data breaks downstream “custom” queries and dashboards |  |
| Sigma permission limitation | Sigma users without edit access cannot view SQL behind widget |  |
| Metric pack refresh failures | When metric pack source table daily refresh run jobs fail, user does not know and system does not re-run job automatically  |  |
| Need more frequent ETL runs than daily | Example: S\&O build queries/dashboards on Parcel prod data and expects real time data, but underlying data is only refreshed daily |  |
| Unclear process to share Parcel data to external partners | There are lots of asks to send Parcel data to Mx and sort partners, using different methods like Sigma dashboards, SFTP exports etc. There doesn’t seem to be a clear recommendation on which approach to use when.  |  |
|  |  |  |
|  |  |  |

## **Mx pain points**

1. [Mike Linshi](mailto:mike.linshi@doordash.com)  
* Query proliferation – I work with several people on S\&O who are capable with SQL and query writing, and it has resulted in LOTS of queries being written, especially against raw/source data tables, and it can lead to human error and inconsistencies. With regards to the tools your team manages, where this manifests itself is a proliferation of shared Mode queries and Sigma dashboards, much of it duplicative, and much for personal use.  
* As part of deprecating old data tables/pipelines or migrating to new DE-certified tables, it is difficult to track down which queries/dashboards use outdated data tables that need to be migrated. And combined with \#1 above, it's the case that people use the old tables. Here is one example – this [ETL Xpress](https://unity.doordash.com/suites/data/etl-express/workflows/update/918) was created by a former contractor on my team, and I am not sure if/how these tables are currently being used in queries or dashboards. We almost definitely want to deprecate this pipeline at some point this quarter or next quarter, and it's hard to know what the implications are, and to determine where it's actively used so we can go in and fix things. We're running into the same problem where S\&O proliferates custom, Mx-specific OTD reports but if we change the core logic we're unable to easily propagate those changes to all the dependent reports

2. [Angeline Guo](mailto:qi.guo2@doordash.com)  
* The new Sigma version doesn't allow users without Edit access to see SQL/datasource behind a widget. This significantly reduced our productivity in understanding data sources outside of our immediately managed dashboard. This also prevents our XFN(e.g. engineer, S\&O) from self-serving SQL.  
* ETL express doesn't allow DS to manually run it. Once scheduled, it can only run at scheduled time, or we need to ping our DE partner who has access to manually run it. However, given ETL express are mainly built and used by DS, having this access block significantly reduced our productivity to update a ETL express pipeline.

3. [Yikai Chen](mailto:yikai.chen@doordash.com)  
* \[UMP\] I've noticed that the metric pack source table daily refresh run jobs occasionally fail. And I only learned that when I try to manual backfill the source table and look into the run logs. Wonder if the system can auto-rerun the failed jobs \- so we don't miss metric data without knowing it.

4. [Vamsee Gottipati](mailto:vamsee.gottipati@doordash.com)  
   *  Real-Time Dashboards: I frequently hear from S\&O about wanting real-time or close to real-time dashboards, especially for Parcel. Currently our Snowflake tables are refreshed daily, which makes sense for historical reporting. But S\&O often builds ad-hoc queries and dashboards using Parcel prod data, which isn’t really meant for reporting. This makes it hard to trust the numbers since we don’t have visibility into the underlying logic (a lot of it comes from engineering event data). It’d be great to have some kind of intra-day ETL setup to make this process smoother and more reliable.  
   * External Data Sharing: Similar issue on the data sharing side. There are lots of asks to send Parcel data to Mx and sort partners, using different methods like Sigma dashboards, SFTP exports etc. There doesn’t seem to be a clear recommendation on which approach to use when. Plus, ownership is scattered across Ax, DE, and S\&O. We’d really benefit from a clearer framework and ownership model here.
